config.title: Berlin Bike Lanes — FAQ
config.subtitle: Quick answers for tourists walking, cycling or using e-scooters in Berlin.

items.q: Can tourists walk in Berlin bike lanes?
items.a: No. Treat marked Berlin bike lanes as active traffic space, not as extra sidewalk. Step fully onto the pedestrian pavement before checking maps, taking photos, waiting for friends or handling luggage.

items.q: Are cyclists allowed to ride on the sidewalk in Berlin?
items.a: Adult cyclists should not ride on the sidewalk unless a sign specifically allows it. Children have limited sidewalk exceptions under German traffic rules, but pedestrians must still not be endangered or obstructed.

items.q: What do red bike lanes in Berlin mean?
items.a: For tourists, the practical answer is: pay attention and stay clear unless you are cycling there. Red or painted lanes often mark bike traffic space, conflict zones or priority areas where stopping as a pedestrian creates problems quickly.

items.q: Can I ride an e-scooter on the sidewalk in Berlin?
items.a: No. Berlin guidance says e-scooters may not be ridden on sidewalks or pedestrian zones. Use cycle infrastructure where it exists, or the road where required, and park without blocking pedestrians, ramps, doors, crossings or bike lanes.

items.q: Can I take a bicycle on Berlin public transport?
items.a: Usually yes on regional trains, S-Bahn, U-Bahn and trams in marked areas when there is space, but you need the correct bike ticket. Wheelchairs and strollers have priority, and crowded services may not be practical.

items.q: Do I need a bike for the BerlinWalk tour?
items.a: No. The BerlinWalk route is on foot and lasts **2 hours**. It is a good first orientation before you decide whether a rental bike, e-scooter, public transport or walking makes sense for the rest of your day.
